actions.thought: K9o is a weak offsuit king that is generally a fold from under the gun, even in a 4-handed game (where UTG is equivalent to the Cutoff). It's the first hand of the tournament, and I prefer to wait for stronger opening hands rather than playing a marginal hand out of position against three opponents.

I'm on the button with K♣4♠ after Flash folded from UTG. While K4o is a weak hand, I'm in an excellent position to apply pressure. The pot is 150, and I'm facing only the blinds (50 + 100). 

A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard button play here and accomplishes several objectives:
1. Generates strong fold equity - the blinds will often fold weaker holdings, allowing me to win the pot immediately
2. If called, I retain positional advantage for the entire hand and can outplay my opponent post-flop
3. It's appropriate bet sizing for this spot with my hand strength and position

Early in a tournament, stealing blinds is valuable for stack growth. This is a standard, profitable play from the button.

Claude button opened to 300 and I’m in the small blind with J♣T♦ (offsuit). With ~99 BB effective, JTo is strong enough to defend vs a standard button open but too marginal for a light 3-bet out of position. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable and play the flop, committing the 250 more chips (300 total) rather than inflate the pot OOP with a weak 3-bet.

Preflop in the big blind with 8h5s, a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against ranges. Claude raised to 300 from the button (3x raise), and Minni called from the small blind, indicating strength. Pot is 700, calling 200 gives ~3.5:1 odds, but 85o has terrible equity multiway against these ranges (button open ~30-40% wide, SB call tighter). No position postflop, stack deep (~100BB), better to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term in tournament.

Check. JTo on K-6-4 rainbow is a marginal hand out of position — no pair, only two overcards and only backdoor club/diamond possibilities. I want to keep the pot under control and see how the button reacts. Checking preserves fold equity against bluffs and lets me call reasonable continuation bets to realize equity, but avoids committing OOP with a hand that performs poorly versus a strong C-bet. Plan: call smaller c-bets (~≤1/3 pot ≈ 225–250) to see a turn, fold to larger c-bets (~≥1/2 pot ≈ 350+) unless I improve, and only apply aggression if I pair or pick up a real draw. I will not lead or check-raise here with this holding.

Top pair on a dry board after Minni checked. Her check from small blind indicates weakness. A standard c-bet builds the pot while I'm likely ahead and generates fold equity. Betting 450 (roughly 2/3 of the 700 pot) is standard continuation bet sizing that balances value and fold equity.

Claude's flop bet is 450 into a 700 pot (~64% pot). Calling 450 to win a 1600 pot requires ~28% equity. With JcTd on K-6-4 rainbow I have no pair and only very weak backdoor draws (I need two cards to make a straight or a backdoor flush). Against a button C-bet range here (Kx, big pairs, some bluffs), my realistic equity is well below the required 28%, and I'm out of position for the rest of the hand. I previously planned to call small c-bets but fold to larger ones (≥~1/2 pot) unless I improve. Given the size, lack of real draw, and deep stacks to preserve for better spots, folding is the correct play.

UTG position requires a tight opening range. J♥9♠ (Jack-Nine offsuit) is a speculative hand that doesn't meet the standard criteria for early position play. From UTG, I should be opening with premium pairs (JJ+) and strong broadway cards (AK, AQ, AJ). J9 lacks the strength needed to open here, especially with three aggressive opponents behind me. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities while I establish disciplined play early in the tournament. This is a standard fold from UTG.

9♣2♣ is a very weak hand. Although I'm on the button, I'm facing the small and big blind with deep stacks — this will often be a multiway pot and I'll be out of position postflop. Calling 100 to play a weak, poorly connected suited gutter hand heads into difficult decisions with low equity, and raising as a steal is risky because both blinds can defend wide with deep stacks. Fold and preserve chips for better spots.

Folded to me in the small blind with A4s, a suited ace with decent playability and blockers. This is a standard spot to raise for dead money (150 in pot) and to steal the big blind or take initiative OOP. BB defends wide but A4s has good equity against his calling range and postflop potential (flush/straight draws, ace high). Raising to 2.5x BB (250 total) is a balanced size early in the tournament with deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. The Small Blind (Elon) has raised to 250. I need to put in 150 more to call, getting approximately 2.6:1 on my money. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide. Because I will have position for the remainder of the hand, King-high has enough equity and playability to warrant a defense here rather than folding. Calling is the standard play to see a flop and evaluate.

Preflop, I raised with A4o from the SB as a standard steal/initiative play. BB Flash called, which gives him a wide range including Ax, Kx, pairs, suited connectors, and diamond draws. The flop Ks6d2d is decent for my range: I can have AK/AQ/AJ, bluffs like suited aces, and overplays. This board hits his calling range (Kx, 65s, diamond draws) but my ace blocker is valuable, and I have initiative OOP. With deep stacks early in the tournament, a continuation bet maintains pressure, gets value from weaker Ax/draws, and has fold equity against his floats/marginal calls. Checking invites him to bet his equity-heavy hands, putting me in tough spots OOP. Betting 60% pot (300 into 500) balances value and bluff frequencies.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair on a board that contains a flush draw. In a blind-versus-blind encounter, my hand is extremely strong. I am raising for value, targeting Elon's potential King-high hands (AK, KQ, KJ) and charging any diamond draws. Raising also helps protect my hand before any dangerous cards come on the turn.

Facing a flop 3-bet from Flash in the BB after defending my SB steal-raise preflop with A4o and a standard c-bet. My hand is ace-high with no pair, no draws, and minimal blockers (Ac blocks some Ax but not KJ/KQ effectively here). The board Ks6d2d is draw-heavy (diamond flush draw, gutshots), hitting his wide defending range hard: top pair Kx, sets (unlikely), flush draws (AdXd, KdXd), and combo draws. His 3-bet sizing to 900 (3x my bet into 800 pot) into a 1700 pot now requires calling 600 OOP (35% equity needed, but I have ~10-20% vs his value range, less vs draws). Deep stacks early (9k+ effective), but folding bluffs to resistance is standard discipline OOP; no fold equity on turn, facing aggression from a wide range that now polarizes strong post-3bet. Previous dynamics show Flash folding to my aggression in Hand 1 indirectly, but here he fights back correctly. Save stack for better spots; this is a clear fold to maintain balanced ranges.

Holding 4♦7♠ UTG against a raise, this is a clear fold. The hand is weak, unsuited and frequently dominated by typical open-raise ranges. I’m out of position to the button/SB/BB and there’s no compelling equity or nut-draw potential to justify calling 100 or risking a min-raise to 200 as a bluff early in the tournament. With ~97bb effective I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot in position or with real equity.

T3o is a trash hand with poor playability post-flop, no strong draws or pairs possible easily. On the button with dead UTG, blinds might defend, but early in tournament (hand 3/150), deep stacks, no need to risk chips on a speculative steal with such a weak hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt to steal the Big Blind with a raise, J5o is a very weak hand to play out of position if Claude calls. Given the early stage of the session and the poor quality of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Pocket fives (55) is a strong hand UTG in a 4-handed game with deep stacks (~90-100BB effective). Early in the tournament (hand 4/150), we want to play aggressively with premium pairs to build the pot and thin the field. Limping is weak and invites multi-way pots where 55 is vulnerable. Standard open-raise size is 2.5-3x the BB; previous hands show 250-300, so 300 (3x) is appropriate to balance value and fold equity without overcommitting.

I am holding 93o on the button. Elon opened from UTG to 300. 93o is an extremely weak hand and does not play well against an UTG opening range. Given the tournament context and my stack size, there is no reason to defend or bluff with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

This is 3-7 offsuit, widely considered the worst starting hand in poker. Elon raised to 300 from UTG (early position), indicating a reasonably strong holding. Even though I'm getting approximately 2.8:1 pot odds (450 current pot + 250 to call), folding weak hands to aggression is fundamental sound poker strategy. 3-7o has minimal equity against a range of hands that would raise from UTG, and playing it would only contribute to negative expected value over a tournament. Position and hand strength both suggest folding is the optimal play. I'll preserve chips for premium hands where I have a clearer edge.

I have pocket 8s (8♠8♦) in the big blind facing an UTG open to 300. With ~96bb effective stacks this is a clear candidate for a value 3-bet: pocket eights are ahead of much of an open-raising range and I want to isolate Elon, take the initiative, and deny cheap overcard flops. I’ll raise to 1,000 total (≈10bb) — big enough to extract value and fold out some weaker broadways but small enough to keep worse hands in and leave room to maneuver postflop. If Elon 4-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large shove; consider calling smaller 4-bets), and if he calls I’ll play carefully OOP and c-bet appropriate flops.

Pocket 5s remains a strong hand facing a BB 3-bet after my UTG open. Minni's 3-bet range from BB is polarized: premium hands like QQ+, AK, or bluffs/light 3-bets exploiting position. Calling 700 more (total 1000) offers excellent pot odds (700 into 1350, pot becomes 2050) and strong implied odds with 90BB+ effective stacks for set-mining. 55 has ~12% equity vs typical 3-bet range and plays well multi-way or heads-up postflop. Folding is too tight early with deep stacks (only 3% stack committed); 4-betting risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand unlikely to get value/fold-out better ranges. Plan: Call, see flop in position? Wait no, I'm UTG, OOP postflop, but still +EV. Build pot selectively over 150 hands.

I 3‑bet preflop to isolate with 8♠8♦ and Elon just called. Flop A♥ J♥ Q♠ is very bad for an underpair: no pair, no real draws, and I’m out of position. Elon’s UTG‑call range to my 3‑bet includes lots of hands that hit this board (A‑x, KQ, QJ, Jx, big pairs) so my fold equity is low and a c‑bet will frequently be called or raised by hands that already beat me. The correct line is pot control: check and let him have the initiative. I’ll fold to meaningful aggression but can consider calling a very small probe to see a turn if the price is cheap; if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn for a delayed bluff on a blank or to fold quietly. For now: check.

Preflop call with 55 was correct set-mining play deepstacked early in tournament. Flop AhJhQs is a complete whiff: no pair, no draws, underpair to board. Minni (BB) checked first postflop, giving me position. Her 3-bet range hits this board hard (AQo/s, AJs, KQ, ATs flush draw possible, QQ+), so betting into it as a bluff risks calls from better hands or check-raises from monsters, with poor equity (~20-30% vs her range) and no fold equity against polarized range that checks weak/misses. Checking back controls pot, realizes equity cheaply if she has air, allows pot control OOP if turn worsens, and invites bluffs on later streets where I can value-own or fold. With deep stacks (80+BB), no need to bluff prematurely in hand 4/150; preserve stack for better spots.

I’m OOP with pocket eights on a highly coordinated board (A♥ J♥ Q♠ T♦). Elon checked the flop and turn; his calling/flatting range to my 3‑bet contains many hands that already beat me (A‑x, Jx, Qx, T‑pairs, and any K is a made Broadway straight on this turn). I only have two outs to improve to a set (~4% equity) and very little fold equity — a turn bet is unlikely to fold better hands and invites a check‑raise or a commitment with a dominated but still-better holding. The correct, low-variance line is pot control: check and see the river, folding to meaningful aggression but considering a call to a very small probe. If checked through I’ll re-evaluate the river for a possible delayed bluff on a safe blank; otherwise preserve stack and avoid bloating the pot with an underpair.

Pocket 5s remains a bluff-catcher at best on this highly coordinated AhJhQsTd board, with straights (e.g., KTs for Broadway), flush draws (hearts), and top pairs dominating Minni's polarized BB 3-bet range. She checked flop and turn, which could be weakness (missed bluffs) or trapping monsters (AA, AQ, sets). OOP with no showdown value or draws, betting risks raising out equity (~25-35% vs. her range) into callers or check-raises, with low fold equity against a range that checks back air or strong hands. Checking back realizes equity cheaply, controls the pot size deepstacked (80+BB), induces bluffs on river if we brick, and preserves stack early (hand 4/150) for premium spots. Preflop set-mining was +EV; now play passively post-oak to minimize losses.

Board: A♥ J♥ Q♠ T♦ T♣. I hold 8♠8♦ — an underpair with no straight or flush. Both players checked flop and turn so we never got to take the initiative, but the river pairing the Ten makes my hand essentially worthless: any T in a villain’s hand is now trips (or could be a full house if combined with A/J/Q), and many hands in Elon’s flatting range (A‑x, Jx, Qx, KQ, KJ, some two‑card straights like K9) beat or very likely call a bluff. My fold equity is extremely low and a bluff here would be thin and often called by A‑high/broadway hands. With pot = 2050 and no bet to call, the lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and fold to meaningful river aggression. I’ll check and preserve my stack rather than bluff into a heavy range that dominates me.

Pocket 5s on AhJhQsTdTc board is a pure bluff-catcher with minimal showdown value, beaten by any 10 (trips), top pair (A,J,Q), straights (KT, KT), and better pairs. Minni's BB 3-bet range (QQ+, AK, bluffs) hits this board frequently, but her triple check suggests possible weakness (missed draws, air) or trapping slowplays. OOP on river with no bets, betting as a bluff has poor fold equity vs her checking range (strong hands check back, bluffs might call or raise); risks losing to calls from better hands while realizing 0 equity if raised. Checking realizes equity cheaply against bluffs she might hero-call with? No, she can't bet after checking first? Wait, she checked river first (as IP? Action order suggests she acts first postflop? But anyway), checking back preserves deep stack (83BB), controls pot OOP, induces potential showdown where 55 beats pure air ~20-30% of her weak checking range. +EV preflop call led here; now passive play minimizes variance early (hand 4/150). No value bet, no profitable bluff spot.

I am dealt 8d 5h in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak offsuit hand that does not perform well in multi-way pots or against strong ranges. Opening with such a hand from UTG is unprofitable and unnecessarily risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. While I'm on the button with reasonable pot odds (150 to call 100), this hand lacks sufficient equity against a competent field. The small blind and big blind will likely continue with far superior holdings if I call. Even in position, 3-2o cannot generate profitable long-term value. Folding here is the mathematically correct play and aligns with GTO strategy for weak hands in this position.

Heads-up from the small blind with 9♠6♠ — a suited connector with decent postflop playability. In heads-up play I want to take the initiative and deny the big blind cheap odds, so I’ll raise to a standard 2xBB (200 total). This keeps the pot manageable while pressuring marginal hands and lets me realize equity if called. If Elon 3-bets large I’ll likely fold; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously OOP.

T4o is a speculative offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low equity against a SB opening range, which is wide but still favors folding trash OOP. Pot odds of 3:1 aren't enough given the terrible postflop prospects and deep stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack for better opportunities early in the tournament.

Q7o is a weak hand that should be folded from UTG regardless of stack sizes or game dynamics. While we're in a 4-handed game with increased aggression, UTG remains the worst position to play marginal hands from. I have no information about opponent intentions, and Q7o performs poorly in multi-way pots and is difficult to play out of position. With a healthy 10400 chip stack, I can afford to wait for premium hands in better positions. Standard strategy dictates folding weak holdings from early position to maintain a tight, profitable range.

On the button with 9♥6♣ I have a marginal but playable hand — position is the real weapon here. With only the blinds to act and decent fold-frequency shown previously (Elon has folded to my opens), I want to take the initiative and avoid giving cheap odds for multiway pots. A standard open to 3x the big blind (300 total) pressures the blinds, preserves fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position. If I face a large 3-bet I can release this hand; vs calls I can use position to realize equity and apply pressure.

Preflop from the small blind facing a button min-raise to 300 after UTG fold. Pot is 450, to call 250 more. A4 suited clubs is a strong hand here: ace blocker, suited, good playability. Button range is wide (any two cards often). Equity realization is solid vs that range (~45-50% equity). Deep stacks, but squeezing is profitable with dead SB/BB money and position on button. BB likely to fold often. Raising to 900 (3x her raise) applies pressure, denies equity to worse aces/hands. Calling OOP risks tough spots; folding too tight. Standard squeeze spot.

I am holding Js 7h in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button, and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. Js 7h is a weak hand that does not play well against a 3-bet range. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal hand against two opponents (assuming Minni might call). Folding is the clear and disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Fold. Facing Elon's sizable 3‑bet to 900 after my 300 open, 9♥6♣ is too weak to continue. The hand is poorly connected and often dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, suited connectors), and my post‑flop playability is very poor even in position. Calling 600 into the ~1300 pot only gets ~31–32% pot odds, which is marginal against a value‑heavy 3‑bet range; a min 4‑bet to 1500 as a bluff is too thin and risks committing chips with a largely drawing/non‑playable hand. I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I’m UTG in a 4-handed game with A♣J♣ — a strong, playable hand that wants to take the initiative. With deep stacks (~105bb) and only three opponents, I should open rather than limp to build the pot and get fold equity. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300). This sizing pressures the blinds (Flash has shown a tendency to fold to big preflop raises; Claude may defend; Elon is capable of 3-bets), narrows the field, and gives me position/control postflop if I get called. Postflop plan: continuation-bet many favorable boards (and any ace or club), play cautiously when heavily 3-bet (call small 3-bets to see flops, fold to very large 3-bets unless reads suggest otherwise), and extract value from worse aces and club draws.

Preflop, UTG raise from Minni to 300 into 450 pot. Holding 3c4c suited connectors on the button with deep stacks (~86BB). While position is good and Minni has been raising frequently (folded to my 3bet in hand 6), 34s is too weak against an UTG opening range, even if somewhat loose. Equity realization postflop is marginal without multiway action, and pot odds are only about 2.5:1. Direct fold to preserve stack early in tournament (hand 7/150). No value to raise or call profitably long-term.

Q7 suited is a marginal hand to play from the Small Blind against an UTG open. Although the pot odds are okay, playing out of position for the rest of the hand against Minni, and with the Big Blind still to act, makes this a clear fold. I prefer to conserve my stack for more advantageous situations.

Folding 26o (deuce-six offsuit) from the big blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker and has virtually no postflop equity. Even though pot odds appear reasonable at 2.25:1 (calling 200 to win 450), I lack the minimum ~31% equity needed to call profitably. Additionally, being out of position in the big blind against an UTG raiser from Minni is particularly disadvantageous for marginal hands. With 150 hands ahead and a healthy 10,300 chip stack, this is an excellent spot to fold and preserve chips for premium holdings. Early tournament aggression should be reserved for hands with actual value.

7h3c is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated offsuit with no high card value or playability. UTG in a 4-handed game still requires a tight opening range to avoid difficult postflop situations, especially deepstacked (~85BB). Standard play is to fold trash like this preflop to preserve stack and wait for better spots. No reads or dynamics justify entering the pot here.

I am on the button with Q9o. In a 4-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Elon has already folded. Minni in the big blind has been active, but Q9o is strong enough to open for 2.5x the big blind.

Ad9h is a solid hand with multiple avenues to win - ace-high, straight draws, and broadway connectivity. Flash's 250 raise from the button is a standard positional raise with a wide range. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (200 to call into a 600 pot), which is favorable for a hand with decent equity and playability post-flop. Being out of position, I prefer to keep the pre-flop pot manageable and play this hand on the flop rather than commit more chips pre-flop. This position allows Minni to act in the BB, which could provide additional information. Calling is ideal - I maintain flexibility to fold if the board is unfavorable or advance if I hit my hand.

I have As7o in the big blind facing a button open to 250 with the small blind calling. 3-betting here is marginal — multiway pot reduces the efficacy of a light 3-bet and I’d be out of position versus the button postflop. The call is cheap: I must put in 150 more into a 600 pot (150/(600+150)=20% equity needed), so the pot odds and implied odds justify seeing a flop with an Ace-high that blocks many button Ace combos. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with top pair or strong draws, pot-control or fold to strong aggression if I miss. If I face a raise preflop I’ll fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing.

Betting 400 chips on this coordinated flop with top pair and a gutshot draw. I'm likely ahead of Flash's button-raising range and need to extract value while I'm ahead. This sizing is proportional to pot (50%) and maintains control while building the pot.

I have A♠7♣ on a T♦ A♣ K♠ flop — top pair but a very weak kicker. Claude (SB) called preflop and now bets 400 into a 750 pot (pot is 1150 after the bet); I must call 400 to continue. Pot odds: 400 to win 1550 ≈ 25.8% equity required. Given the small bet size, the multiway dynamics (Flash, the original raiser, still to act), and my poor kicker (I am often behind other A‑x combos or crushed by two pair/sets/straights like QJ), a shove/large raise here would be inviting trouble and bloating the pot out of position. Calling is the prudent play: my top pair has enough equity to justify a call; I keep the pot controlled and let the button act next so I can re-evaluate based on further action. If I face a substantial raise after calling I will fold to big aggression (since many better Aces/two pair beat me); if opponents check or call, I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and extract value only from worse A‑x or Kx hands.

The flop T-A-K is extremely coordinated and hits the calling ranges of both the Small Blind (Claude) and Big Blind (Minni) very hard. Claude led into the field and Minni called, suggesting they both have significant pieces of this board (likely an Ace, King, or a straight draw). I currently have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). However, if a Jack arrives, it completes a broadway straight that any player holding a Jack or even just a Queen would share. My 9-kicker is useless. Given the action, I am likely drawing thin or dead, and I am not getting the necessary pot odds (400 to win 1950) to continue with a weak gutshot. Raising as a bluff into two players who have shown strength on an Ace-high board is also ill-advised.

On the turn with board Td Ac Ks Th, I have two pair (Aces and Tens with a 9 kicker), which is a strong made hand. The pairing of the board is notable - it could have helped Minni if they held Tens (set), broadway cards JQ (straight), or helped with their top pair holdings from the flop. However, my two pair still represents significant strength.

Minni called my 400 flop bet on a coordinated board, indicating they likely have a hand worth defending - top pair (AK, KQ), a pair, or possibly draws. Given my hand strength and position, a value bet is appropriate here. A 600 bet (roughly 39% of the 1550 pot) is sized proportionally to build the pot while not overcommitting if I'm facing a strong hand. This bet:
1. Extracts value from hands I beat (AQ, weaker holdings)
2. Builds the pot when I'm likely ahead
3. Sets up river decisions based on Minni's response
4. Maintains pot control in a profitable spot

The turn card didn't reduce my hand's strength materially - if anything, it should concern hands that were drawing, while my made two pair remains a strong holding.

Pot is 2150 after Claude’s 600 turn bet; calling 600 requires roughly 28% equity. I hold A♠7♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker. The turn paired the Ten, increasing the likelihood of trips/full houses or two‑pair (T, Kx, A‑K) that beat me; Claude’s line (call preflop, small c‑bet on flop, small turn bet) looks like a capped/value/c‑bet range rather than a narrow polarized monster range. Raising out of position with a weak kicker would bloat the pot and often get called or 3‑bet by hands that dominate me, so I keep the pot controlled. Plan: call 600, see the river, fold to large raise/shove, call small river bets for value or check back if Claude shows weakness.

River value bet with the nuts. I hold a full house (Aces full of Tens) - the best possible hand on this board. Minni's calling pattern through the flop and turn (called 400, then called 600) indicates they have a legitimate made hand, most likely AK, AQ, or pocket pairs (KK, TT, QQ). All of these hands lose to my full house. A 1000 chip bet (36% of the 2750 pot) is optimal here - it's substantial enough to extract value from the hands Minni will call with, while not being so large that it appears suspicious or induces a fold. This is pure value extraction with the best hand.

I have A♠7♣ and the river Td Ac Ks Th Ah gives me A-A-A-T-T — a full house (Aces full of Tens). It’s heads‑up vs Claude, who has been calling and giving small-to-medium value bets (400 flop, 600 turn, 1000 river). The only realistic hands that beat me are quads tens (pocket Tens); a remaining single ace (Ad) would only chop. Those combos are rare given the action (Flash folded on the flop, etc.). Pot is 3750 and he bet 1000; calling is clearly +EV (you only need ~21% equity), but I want to extract maximum value from worse hands (Kx, two pair, sets, A‑x that ties) that will call a raise. I will raise for value to the minimum allowed (2,000 total) to get called by thin value hands while keeping the sizing reasonable — if he shoves I will call (only quads really beat me).

I'm facing a river raise from 1000 to 2000 with Ad-9h on a board of Td-Ac-Ks-Th-Ah.

Hand Analysis: I hold a full house (Aces full of Tens) - Ad, Ac, Ah, Th, Td. This is an elite hand that beats nearly all holdings except four of a kind (a very specific hand like pocket TT).

Pot Odds: I'm being offered 7750 to call 1000 (7.75:1), meaning I only need to win ~12.9% of the time to break even.

Minni's Range Analysis: Based on their call pattern through flop (400), turn (600), and now river raise to 2000, they likely have:
- AK (two pair on river, with 2 aces showing)
- Pocket KK (set on flop, loses to my full house)
- QJ (broadway straight, loses to my full house)
- Possibly bluffing with a hand like AQ or KQ

The probability Minni has pocket TT (the only hand that beats me with four of a kind) is relatively low. They'd need to have started with that hand preflop and made the correct calls, which is possible but not the most likely scenario.

Action Decision: CALL. My hand strength combined with favorable pot odds makes calling the mathematically sound decision. Folding a full house to a river raise would only be justified if I had extreme reason to believe Minni has four of a kind, which I don't based on the action sequence. Calling maximizes value against the broader range of hands Minni could have.

I am in the UTG position with 4c 9s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. In a four-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider, but 94-offsuit is still well below the threshold for a profitable open. Folding is the only sensible play here to conserve chips for better opportunities.

5-10 offsuit is a weak, bottom-tier holding that doesn't justify entering the pot, even from the button. While position is valuable in poker, it doesn't overcome the fundamental weakness of this hand. At 100 chips to call, this would be a long-term unprofitable play. I have 150 hands to accumulate chips and should be disciplined about hand selection, saving my aggression for stronger holdings and more favorable situations. Folding preserves my chip stack for better opportunities ahead.

Heads-up from the small blind with T3o (3h Ts) — a marginal, often dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Critical river decision analysis on board 7c 5d Kh 8d 7d with TT:

**River raise interpretation**: Minni's action pattern has been calling my aggressive bets on flop (450), turn (750), and then river raise to 1600. This represents a significant shift from passive calling to active raising - a strong polarized signal.

**Board context**: The river 7 is precisely the card that completes two pair for most King combinations in her range. Given her three-street commitment pattern (totaling ~1950 chips in calls), she almost certainly holds a King, and that King now makes two pair (K7) which beats my one pair of tens.

**Range analysis**: 
- Her calling three streets indicates likely holdings: KQ, KJ, KT, K9, K8, K7
- The river 7 dramatically improves all King holdings to two pair
- If she held 77 or any King, the river brought her a made hand
- Her river raise suggests confidence in a strong holding, not a thin value bet or bluff

**Equity calculation**: I need ~25% equity to call 1600 into the 4750 pot. Against her likely King-heavy range now holding two pair, I estimate only 15-20% equity. The unfavorable board texture and her raise signal indicate I'm behind more often than not.

**Fold rationale**: While TT is a reasonable showdown hand, the specific river card (another 7), combined with her shift to aggressive action after passive calls, indicates she made her hand. Folding minimizes loss in a situation where my hand is beaten by her most likely holdings.

This is a disciplined fold recognizing that the river improved her range significantly more than it helped my holding.

A 700 raise builds the pot to ~1,050 when I'm ahead of most of her button-raising range and creates good fold equity against weaker holdings while generating value from stronger hands.

I opened to 300 on the button and Claude 3-bet to 700. I hold T5o — an off‑suit, marginal hand that fares poorly against a typical BB 3‑bet range of high pairs and broadways. I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c 5h). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or isolate one opponent. This standard 3x raise is effective for building a pot while maintaining control of the hand from position.

Folding A7d from the small blind facing Flash's button raise. In short-handed poker, conserving chips by folding borderline hands out of position against aggressive opponents is a sound strategy, especially when I have 10k+ chips and can wait for better spots. The risk/reward doesn't favor playing here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button heads-up. I have Kd Tc — a good hand to defend in the big blind but marginal as a polarized 3-bet OOP. Check. On 8h-6s-5s with Kd-Tc I have two overcards and only remote backdoor straight outs, no spade, so very little immediate equity. Betting here as a bluff has limited fold equity and would commit me to difficult decisions out of position; a check-raise bluff is too risky with no real draw. Plan: check now, call small (~≤1/3 pot) c-bets to float, fold to large sizing, and reevaluate on the turn.

I have flopped bottom set on a coordinated board (8h 6s 5s). While I have a very strong hand, the board offers several straight and flush draws. Minni's check-call pre-flop suggests a wide range. I will bet 450 (approx 70% of the pot) to get value from top pair, middle pair, and various draws while protecting my equity.

I defended preflop with KTd to keep the pot controllable and checked the flop to exercise pot control. On 8h-6s-5s I have no pair, no spade and only weak backdoor outs. Per my plan to call small c-bets but fold to heavy aggression, I will fold and preserve my stack for better, positional spots.

5-4 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in poker - even on the button. While position is valuable for stealing, this hand lacks the equity and playability needed. 3♠2♥ is one of the weakest hands to play out of the small blind. Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to see the flop (break-even equity ≈25%). I’m on the button with K♦8♥ in a three-handed game (lots of fold equity). With 63 BB I have plenty of maneuvering room. A standard 3x-ish open keeps the field honest, reduces the range of callers (vs a min-raise which invites lots of calls/3-bets) and preserves post‑flop options. If I get called I can continuation-bet many dry boards; if I face a 3-bet I can re-evaluate against the opponent and stack depth. I’ll raise to a size consistent with previous opens to apply pressure and take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind holding 7 of Diamonds and 5 of Clubs. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. 75 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially when out of position against the original raiser and with the Big Blind still left to act. Calling or raising with such a weak hand would likely lead to losing more chips in the long run. Folding is the most prudent action.

Raising with ATo against short-stacked Minni's button raise. The hand has solid equity (~45-50%) against their wide range, pot odds favor a raise, and I can exploit their chip disadvantage. A raise to 700 applies meaningful pressure on their 6,000 stack while maintaining reasonable risk/reward. I'll have positional advantage post-flop if called.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♦8♥ and Claude (BB) 3-bet to 700. In a three-handed pot his 3-bet range is likely wider than in full-ring and he’s shown regular aggression, but K8o is off‑suit and often dominated by his value hands. With ~60 BB effective and position, calling is the best line: the call gives good pot odds (~400 into ~1450), lets me realize equity and use position to c‑bet favorable flops (or fold to heavy action when missed). Plan postflop: c‑bet/pressure on dry boards and on K; play cautiously vs heavy resistance. Therefore I’ll call the 3‑bet and see a flop.

I have AcTd on a 7s-Ks-Qc flop. I hold top pair with a decent kicker, and I have positional advantage. Minni is short-stacked (~5600) and already committed 700 pre-flop by calling my raise. The broadway-heavy flop is more likely to have hit my range than a button raiser's typical calling range. A 900 bet (roughly 62% of the pot) is appropriately aggressive: it builds value from weaker pairs (pocket sevens, unpaired broadway cards), applies pressure on Minni's short stack, and continues the aggression from pre-flop. This sizing is standard and maintains good risk/reward while keeping Minni in a tight spot. Flash is not in the hand, so I only need to navigate Minni's decision.

Claude 3‑bet me preflop so his range is weighted toward value, but his flop sizing (900 into a 2350 pot) is small and can include a lot of hands (weak Kx, Qx floats, backdoor spade draws and bluffs). I have top pair with a mediocre 8 kicker — enough equity to continue in position but not a hand I want to bloat the pot with by raising. Calling preserves pot control, gets the correct price (~900 into 2350 ≈ 28% required equity) to realize against his wide continuation range, and lets me use position to extract value or fold to heavy turn aggression. Plan: call the 900, re‑evaluate on the turn (bet for value vs weak K/Q hands or check/call against further pressure; fold to clear strength such as large check‑raises or a shove if the turn changes the texture dramatically).

Betting 2000 on the turn with a pair of tens and ace kicker. Minni is short-stacked (4700 remaining) and has already committed significant chips. This bet applies pressure on their stack while extracting value from their calling range (weaker pairs, ace-high holdings, and broadway cards without premium strength). A call leaves them with ~2700, making a river decision difficult if I continue aggression. This sizing is aggressive but balanced - it represents strength consistent with my flop action while allowing Minni to make a decision that likely results in them being pot-committed regardless. If Minni shoves, the pot odds and my equity with a pair justify a call.

I have top pair (K) with a weak 8 kicker on a very wet turn (Ks Qc Ts 7s — three spades). Claude 3-bet preflop and has shown aggression postflop, so his range is weighted to strong broadways and pairs but also contains floats, spade semi‑bluffs and some bluffs. Pot odds to call are attractive: 2000 into 5250 (~28% equity required). Calling preserves pot control and keeps worse Kx and some Qx/Jx floats in the pot while using my position to fold to clear strength on the river. Raising here would be too thin — the board completes many hands that already crush me (straights, two‑pair, sets, flushes) and a raise would commit a lot of chips with a vulnerable kicker. Folding is too passive given the odds and that many hands I beat will continue. Plan: call 2000, see the river, and then re-evaluate (fold to a shove if the river action and texture make it clear I'm behind; extract value vs weaker Kx/Qx if checked to me).

Value betting pair of tens on the river against short-stacked Minni. They've called three aggressive streets and have only 2700 remaining. This bet puts them all-in and extracts maximum value from their likely calling range (broadway cards, weaker pairs). The river has no further runout, and my pair represents strength consistent with my aggressive line throughout the hand. Minni's call rate should be high given pot odds and their desperation as a short stack, making this a standard value bet.

Board: 7s Ks Qc Ts Qh. I hold K♦8♥ (top pair, weak kicker). Pot = 9,950; to call = 2,700 (my entire stack). Calling gives me 2,700 into a 12,650 final pot → I need ≈21.3% equity to break even. Claude 3-bet preflop and has barreled flop and turn; his river bet after the Q pairs the board makes his line much more value-heavy: any Q in his hand is now trips/full house, KQ is a full house, sets (TT, QQ, KK), straights (J9) and spade-flushes are all plausible given his aggression and the board texture. The hands I beat are mostly bluffs and rare worse Kx (K2–K7) — I expect far fewer of those than value hands. Given the river pairing dramatically improves Claude’s range, my actual equity is well below the ~21% needed, and calling an all-in here is a marginal gamble that likely costs my tournament life. I should preserve 27 BB, fold, and wait for a better spot. Therefore: fold.

I am holding 7-4 offsuit on the button. This is a very weak hand. QJ offsuit is a solid broadway hand that plays well in heads-up. Minni is severely short-stacked at 2600 chips and likely in push-fold mode. I’m the big blind (2600 stacks, ~26 BB) facing a 300 open from the small blind. Strong position with Ac9c on the button. Flash has a large stack (23,025) but I'm in position which is crucial. This is a standard, profitable play from the button in this configuration.

I’m on the small blind with Qh5d facing a button open to 300 (I have 50 in; to call = 250). Q5 offsuit is a poor, often‑dominated hand with very little post‑flop playability out of position. I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Four offsuit (Qs 4c). Claude, on the Button, has raised to 300. I have A♣K♣ on the button — a premium, suited broadway hand with great equity and postflop playability. With only the blinds to act, opening for value and fold equity is standard. I am in the Small Blind with 8d 2d. Minni, who is now short-stacked with only 2,250 chips (about 22.5 BB), has raised to 300 from the Button. I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, there is little merit in playing this hand. Facing a button open to 300 from the Button in a three-handed game with 2♠8♠ in the small blind. With Ts9s on a 7h-6s-4d flop, I have a premium semi-bluff situation. I hold an open-ended straight draw (any 8 or K gives me a made straight - 8 outs, plus backdoor spade flush draw). This is approximately 35-40% equity against most holdings.

Minni's flop check indicates weakness, and their short stack (2,300 remaining) makes them vulnerable to pressure. They called my pre-flop raise from the small blind, but the missed flop combined with their shallow chip count creates an ideal spot to apply aggression.

Betting here accomplishes multiple goals:

Call 400. I’m OOP with 2♠8♠ on 7♥6♠4♦ — a very weak hand but I have a gutshot to a 5 (4 outs) and a runner‑runner spade backdoor. I missed — 8♠2♠ on 7♥-6♠-4♦-9♥ has no pair, no flush (backdoor spade gone) and only a one-card straight draw to a 5 (4 outs, ~8.7% on the river). Pot is 1,500 and my stack is 1,900 (SPR ≈1.27). Betting 900 on the turn with a made pair of nines. The board (7h-6s-4d-9h) is relatively safe - Minni's passive line (checking turn after calling flop) indicates weakness. I hold 8♠2♠ on 7♥-6♠-4♦-9♥ — no pair, the backdoor spade flush is dead after the turn, and I only have a 5 for a straight (4 outs ≈ 8.7% to hit the river). The pot is 2,400 and Claude just bet 900. If called I’m almost always drawing dead. With my shortish stack and virtually no showdown equity, the correct, lowest‑risk play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I’m on the button with T♠2♣ (T2o), 1,900 chips (~19 BB). Pot is 150 and it’s my action with 100 to call; min-raise is 200. In 3-handed play the button should open a wide range — even marginal hands gain value because of position and fold equity. T2o is weak but playable from the button; a standard 2.5–3x BB open is appropriate. Raising to 300 (3x BB) pressures the blinds, preserves post-flop maneuverability, and avoids the passivity of just calling 100 or the vulnerability of a tiny min-raise. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to continue. Decision: raise to 300.

I have pocket nines in the Small Blind, which is a premium starting hand in this three-handed dynamic. Minni is very short-stacked with only 1,600 chips (16 BBs) and has opened from the button. A 3-bet is necessary here to isolate Minni and force the Big Blind (Claude) to fold, or at least pay a premium to see a flop. By raising to 900, I am effectively committing Minni to the pot while protecting my hand against overcards that Claude might hold. If Minni shoves her remaining 1,300, I am priced in to call with this hand.

This is a straightforward fold. I'm holding 9♦5♠ (trash hand) in the big blind facing a 3-bet from Flash (900) after Minni's initial raise to 300.

Folding preserves my 15,125 stack for premium opportunities.

I opened to 300 from the button with T♠2♣ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 900. This is a clear 3‑bet range that heavily dominates T2o (pocket pairs, AK/AQ, strong broadways plus some bluffs). The pot is ~1300 and I must call 600 (≈32% pot odds) — my hand’s equity versus a typical SB 3‑betting range is well below that, and calling leaves me shallow (poor post‑flop playability). Shoving to 1500+ is a thin bluff against a deep, aggressive player who can comfortably call. With a weak, easily dominated hand and better spots to wait for on the button, I fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Nine of spades, which is a very strong hand for three-handed play. My stack is deep, but Minni is extremely short-stacked with only 15 big blinds. Opening for a standard raise puts pressure on both the small blind and the short-stacked big blind while allowing me to play a pot in position with a hand that has great connectivity and flush potential.

I'm in the Small Blind with 5s-Kc, a marginal hand at best. Flash raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni (Big Blind) is extremely short-stacked at 1500 chips. While pot odds are reasonable (450 to call 250), my hand doesn't merit playing against an aggressor out of position. 5-K is too weak - it doesn't connect well, and I'd be playing with a weak kicker. I'm only 108 hands into a 150-hand tournament, so there's plenty of time to accumulate value with better hands.

Fold. 7♣5♦ is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with very poor playability out of position. Flash opened to 300 from the button (3x); with my short-stack (≈15BB) I should be shoving only hands with decent equity or folding to preserve fold equity and pick better spots. Q-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and falls well below the threshold of playable holdings even from the button. The hand lacks both high-card strength and connectivity needed for postflop playability. I should preserve my solid chip position (15075) and fold, waiting for better opportunities where I have a mathematical edge.

Hand: 6♣2♦ in the small blind (I’ve already posted 50). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to call (bringing my total contribution to 100). Heads-up, I’ll have postflop position if we see a flop, and the immediate pot odds are excellent: risking 50 to win 200 (break-even equity ~25%). 6-2o is junk, but calling is a cheap, correct play here — raising or shoving with this hand is unnecessarily risky with my short ~14.5bb stack and gives poor equity vs a BB defend range. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to a significant preflop reraise, and on the flop only continue if I’ve improved or can reasonably take the pot with position.

I have K8o in the big blind. Claude folded on the button, and Minni completed from the small blind. Minni is very short-stacked with only 1400 chips (14 BB). Usually, a complete in this position from such a short stack suggests either a very weak hand or a trap. Since I can see the flop for free and I have a decent high-card hand that plays well in a checked-down pot, I will check to minimize variance against a player who is likely looking for a spot to double up.

I flopped a weak pair (pair of 2s) with a terrible kicker and I'm out of position. With only ~14BB (stack 1400) I want to control the pot and avoid committing chips with a marginal hand. Checking lets me realize equity and see a cheap turn; if Flash leads a small/medium c-bet I'll call to evaluate the turn, but I won't build a big pot or get tangled in a large raise/shove with bottom pair. There are two hearts on board (possible heart equity for Flash), so betting to deny equity is reasonable sometimes, but OOP and with my short stack pot-control/check-call is the lower-variance, correct line here.

I have King-high on a flop of 10-4-2 with a heart draw. Minni completed from the small blind and checked the flop.
